Title: Revolutionary Road
Author: Richard Yates
My Review:
deftly captures the inner life of a craven, petty man as he is asphyxiated by his need to perform masculinity

it sometimes feels like we invented feeling alienated by modernity sometime in the last fifty years; books like offer an inarguable counterweight

I do wish it did a better job of portraying April's interiority; I'd love to hear a different opinion but I was left with the sense that Yates' insights into male psychology far outstripped his understanding of women (though it's hard to say, since the book is quite intentional about putting us in Frank's head before anyone else's)

4/5 would recommend if you got a lot out of Mad Men

one gets the sense that Yates' sharp insights came from deep self-loathing, which would explain why he seems weaker to me when he has to write beyond the limits of his experience (though I certainly don't think writers need to have lived their characters' experiences before they can write them well; still thinking about how to square this)

Title: The Bell
Author: Iris Murdoch
My Review: Iris Murdoch's writing is lucid, intelligent, and pretty. Deeply impressed by how she switches between the perspectives of her three core characters while maintaining a coherent underlying voice, and constantly raising the stakes. Masterful stuff. One of those books where, if you described it to me by its premise I probably wouldn't have much interest, but the whole thing feels so intensely alive—born of such compassion and humour and wit—that it's a thrill to read. God I love books
